hypothesis: When reusable prompt prefixes vary materially in token size, request hit rate will differ from token-weighted hit rate enough to change the inferred cost benefit of a prompt-cache configuration.
claimBoundary: The evidence identifies metric divergence in a controlled LRU and sliding-TTL simulation built from one frozen article-size distribution. It does not measure any provider's private cache residency, latency, routing, or production request popularity.

baselines: Uncached input cost normalized to 1.0 per input token
Request hit rate as the conventional unweighted cache metric
controls: Fixed-size-prefix negative control where request and token hit rates should coincide
Uniform-popularity control where size-rank alignment should have no designed effect
Capacity ablation at 10%, 20%, and 40% of total prefix tokens
